Happy to share my take. I think it’s a little more complicated than that, so I’d rather steel man than straw man. 

On size of government:   
Conservatives \*tend to\* favor smaller government and thus become suspicious of expanding government programs, even when the intentions are good (like USAID). The reason is because conservatives believe that even with the best of intentions, bureaucracy is inherently corruptible due to the inevitable diffusion of responsibility.   
Liberals, on the other hand, are more likely to support the expansion of government programs because they view the tax system a way to collectively raise the standard of living for everyone. Trusting the aims of bureaucracy, they are more likely to minimize or even excuse corruption as long as the intentions of the programs are to produce greater social equity. 

On change:  
Liberals, specifically progressives, tend to label new ideas, inventions, or approaches as innovative and focus on all the ways change could bring out the ideal social paradigm of equality for all. Their bias is that they are much more critical of the traditional way of doing things (hindsight being 20/20) than they are of new ideas, which they increasingly accept with an almost religious fervor (“trans women are women,” anyone?). The obvious problem with this bias is that not all old ideas are bad, and not all new ideas are good.   
Conservatives, on the other hand, have the opposite bias. They are much more likely to be critical of new ideas. The positive of this, and why I love intellectual conservatism so much, is that they tend to be the only ones in the crowd ringing the warning bells while everyone else is nodding in ascent to a really bad “new idea.” At their best, conservatives are the Cassandra’s of the world. At their worst, of course, conservatives can cling to genuinely outmoded traditions out of an irrational fear of change. 

On Policies:  
Here’s where you try to lump way too many things together. Let’s just talk about the underlying theme.   
Liberals are more likely to blame the structure of society, or the environment, for the behaviors of individuals. Conservatives are more likely to emphasize the autonomy and personal responsibility of the individual actor. 

So let’s apply this to crime. Liberal states focus on changing the environment. This could mean anything from —on the good side— community programs aimed at reducing recidivism to —on the shitty side— literally reducing the enforcement of laws to keep offenders out of jail. In Baltimore in the early 2020’s, the city stopped prosecuting drug offenses, trespassing, prostitution, and a host of other crimes they deemed “small.” Do you think the city was safer? (No. It got worse.)

Conservatives, on the other hand, focus on individual accountability. When it comes to crime, their solution is to punish criminals accordingly. The idea is that the punishment for the crime has to be greater than the incentive to commit the crime. The downside to this approach is that, without some change in the environment, new criminals will keep being made. This is why liberal anti-recidivism programs work well… but they work best when paired with conservative tough-on-crime accountability, not lax law enforcement. 

Hope that helps explain my perspective better.

I think the appeal of conception as the starting point is that it provides a bright-line rule that avoids arbitrariness. That's not really a necessary trait of a logically consistent position though. The unifying belief on the other side is that the early embryo that exists at conception and early pregnancy is does not meet the criteria of constitutional personhood equivalent to a born individual and rights like you and I, which for the purposes of your question is enough. 

Obviously, at some point during gestation that change has to occur. When that occurs is where the pro-choice side has wide disagreement. Some think it may be implantation or a heart beat (though these people generally consider themselves pro-life), others may say viability or consciousness, a scant few (as in I've never actually met anyone arguing this) think it's birth. However, the pro-choice side is broadly unified in rejecting the idea that conception automatically grants full constitutional personhood rights equivalent to a born person.

I also think these discussions sometimes conflate biological classification with philosophical and legal personhood. No one is denying that zygotes are biologically human life, but for many that does not alone meet constitutional personhood. It isn't like this is a unique argument either. We already recognize in medicine and law that biological human life and legal/moral personhood are not always treated as identical concepts. For example, someone who receives a traumatic brain injury resulting irreversible loss of higher brain function to most would be effectively gone as a person even if the processes indicative of biological life  continue. You can take the purely biological stance, but it's going to be unconvincing to the majority of people who fundamentally view this as philosophical question.

The decline in birth rates in developed countries has been well-known and observed for the past few decades. In some places, such as Japan, a decline in the birth rate is partially blamed on economic stagnation, disillusionment and apathy.

However, there are well-known causes that are pretty much universal:

* Decrease in infant and child mortality
* Social security safety net
* Birth control
* Female emancipation
* Higher education
* Higher cost of living

Many of these factors tie in together. Women can now access birth control, so they can choose to delay pregnancy. Both men and women frequently attend higher education, so they delay starting a family until they’ve finished higher education. Both men and women want to develop careers, so they delay starting a family until their careers are established. Then, once they’ve had a child or children, the higher cost of living makes raising a child more expensive, and also means that any time off work to care for the child when young is a greater financial sacrifice in terms of lost wages and potentially lost career progress. In other words, the "opportunity cost" of having a child can be high.

A massive decrease in child mortality means that families don’t need to have a lot of children as “insurance” against the inevitable death of some of the children, so people don’t have as many children. Also, the existence of a social security safety net, included age pensions, means that ageing parents aren’t reliant upon children to support them and they don’t need to have as many children.

Finally, for better or worse, children in developed countries (certainly middle-class children) are typically expected to do a whole range of extra-curricular activities to help them develop and make them well-rounded individuals, such as learning an instrument, doing a sport, going to additional classes after school to learn basic programming or such things. These are all expensive and place an additional monetary and time burden on parents and so, as a result, parents choose to focus their resources upon just one or two children, rather than 4, 5 or more children.

So, basically, if the right wants higher birth rates in developed countries, it simply needs to make people poorer and less well-educated and disenfranchise women. Eerily, there seems to be quite a push for that all of that to happen in quite a few states in the US...

TL;DR: I'm not entirely sure where I'm going with all of this other than to say I wish there were a much greater emphasis on long-term planning at the highest levels of leadership across both government and industry.

We're 100% in agreement on crony capitalism. That stuff is incredibly corrosive to the overall health of any industry where it takes root.

We're also aligned on the difference between public- and private-sector long-term planning. Doing serious, professional long-term planning is expensive. Because of that, it wouldn't surprise me if private companies are better at it than public ones and/or  governmental organizations, but not nearly as much better as people often assume.

Have you ever thought about the idea that corporations, as entities, are incentivized to privatize as much profit as possible while socializing as much of the cost and consequence as possible? At its core, it's another version of the tragedy of the commons. One of the clearest examples is toxic waste dumping into public waterways. Proper disposal is expensive. It's often cheaper to dump the waste illegally and then hire lawyers to fight the resulting legal battles. There are plenty of other examples, but that one illustrates the dynamic pretty well.

It's been interesting because in my MBA program we've been discussing the incentives that drive corporate behavior, starting with shareholder supremacy. That idea is increasingly being challenged by stakeholder theory, but stakeholder theory hasn't really been translated into a dominant legal doctrine yet. Shareholder-focused thinking still heavily influences corporate governance structures.

I've also been thinking a lot about industries that perhaps shouldn't be fully subject to market forces. Things like the 16 critical national infrastructure sectors. Some industries are so vital to public functioning and national security that there may need to be a government-backed option to provide a stable baseline level of service.

Take energy, for example. During supply shocks, oil companies can post record profits while consumers face rapidly rising prices. On one hand, that makes perfect sense from a market perspective. Oil is a global commodity, and when supply is constrained, existing suppliers benefit. Those outcomes follow basic market fundamentals.

On the other hand, it's easy to see why many people view that situation as price gouging. The logic of the market and the public perception of fairness are not always the same thing. The same dynamic can be found in almost any commodity market. What is rational from a pricing perspective can still create outcomes that feel deeply unfair to the people experiencing them.

Happy to share my take. I think it’s a little more complicated than that, so I’d rather steel man than straw man. 

On size of government:   
Conservatives \*tend to\* favor smaller government and thus become suspicious of expanding government programs, even when the intentions are good (like USAID). The reason is because conservatives believe that even with the best of intentions, bureaucracy is inherently corruptible due to the inevitable diffusion of responsibility.   
Liberals, on the other hand, are more likely to support the expansion of government programs because they view the tax system a way to collectively raise the standard of living for everyone. Trusting the aims of bureaucracy, they are more likely to minimize or even excuse corruption as long as the intentions of the programs are to produce greater social equity. 

On change:  
Liberals, specifically progressives, tend to label new ideas, inventions, or approaches as innovative and focus on all the ways change could bring out the ideal social paradigm of equality for all. Their bias is that they are much more critical of the traditional way of doing things (hindsight being 20/20) than they are of new ideas, which they increasingly accept with an almost religious fervor (“trans women are women,” anyone?). The obvious problem with this bias is that not all old ideas are bad, and not all new ideas are good.   
Conservatives, on the other hand, have the opposite bias. They are much more likely to be critical of new ideas. The positive of this, and why I love intellectual conservatism so much, is that they tend to be the only ones in the crowd ringing the warning bells while everyone else is nodding in ascent to a really bad “new idea.” At their best, conservatives are the Cassandra’s of the world. At their worst, of course, conservatives can cling to genuinely outmoded traditions out of an irrational fear of change. 

On Policies:  
Here’s where you try to lump way too many things together. Let’s just talk about the underlying theme.   
Liberals are more likely to blame the structure of society, or the environment, for the behaviors of individuals. Conservatives are more likely to emphasize the autonomy and personal responsibility of the individual actor. 

So let’s apply this to crime. Liberal states focus on changing the environment. This could mean anything from —on the good side— community programs aimed at reducing recidivism to —on the shitty side— literally reducing the enforcement of laws to keep offenders out of jail. In Baltimore in the early 2020’s, the city stopped prosecuting drug offenses, trespassing, prostitution, and a host of other crimes they deemed “small.” Do you think the city was safer? (No. It got worse.)

Conservatives, on the other hand, focus on individual accountability. When it comes to crime, their solution is to punish criminals accordingly. The idea is that the punishment for the crime has to be greater than the incentive to commit the crime. The downside to this approach is that, without some change in the environment, new criminals will keep being made. This is why liberal anti-recidivism programs work well… but they work best when paired with conservative tough-on-crime accountability, not lax law enforcement.

The decline in birth rates in developed countries has been well-known and observed for the past few decades. In some places, such as Japan, a decline in the birth rate is partially blamed on economic stagnation, disillusionment and apathy.

However, there are well-known causes that are pretty much universal:

* Decrease in infant and child mortality
* Social security safety net
* Birth control
* Female emancipation
* Higher education
* Higher cost of living

Many of these factors tie in together. Women can now access birth control, so they can choose to delay pregnancy. Both men and women frequently attend higher education, so they delay starting a family until they’ve finished higher education. Both men and women want to develop careers, so they delay starting a family until their careers are established. Then, once they’ve had a child or children, the higher cost of living makes raising a child more expensive, and also means that any time off work to care for the child when young is a greater financial sacrifice in terms of lost wages and potentially lost career progress. In other words, the "opportunity cost" of having a child can be high.

A massive decrease in child mortality means that families don’t need to have a lot of children as “insurance” against the inevitable death of some of the children, so people don’t have as many children. Also, the existence of a social security safety net, included age pensions, means that ageing parents aren’t reliant upon children to support them and they don’t need to have as many children.

Finally, for better or worse, children in developed countries (certainly middle-class children) are typically expected to do a whole range of extra-curricular activities to help them develop and make them well-rounded individuals, such as learning an instrument, doing a sport, going to additional classes after school to learn basic programming or such things. These are all expensive and place an additional monetary and time burden on parents and so, as a result, parents choose to focus their resources upon just one or two children, rather than 4, 5 or more children.

So, basically, if the right wants higher birth rates in developed countries, it simply needs to make people poorer and less well-educated and disenfranchise women. Eerily, there seems to be quite a push for that all of that to happen in quite a few states in the US...

You’re treating “16 weeks” like it was an actual negotiated settlement that would have locked everyone into 16 weeks nationally. It wasn’t, it was just the number attached to one state law that became the vehicle for attacking Roe/Casey.

If pro-choice groups had “accepted” that law, it wouldn’t magically freeze abortion law at 16 weeks. It would just mean that one pre-viability ban went unchallenged, which would invite the next state to try 12, then 10, then 6, then near-total bans. The issue wasn't “is 16 weeks the compromise?”, it was whether the pre-viability protection still existed at all.

So blaming Roe being overturned on “pro-choice people not accepting 16 weeks” is backwards. The 16-week number was incidental. The pro-life legal strategy was to use that case to destroy the constitutional rule underneath it. Once that rule was gone, there was no reason to think the result would stop at 16. That’s exactly why treating it as a clean compromise is not serious.

The first thing you have to understand is that NATO was formed in the wake of WWII ostensibly as a means for the US to control European security policy as a bulwark against the USSR. Bush (Sr) actually talked about and began to rework US foreign policy as the USSR fell apart and ultimately collapsed recognizing that our security interests changed the moment the USSR was no longer a serious threat as a world power. Unfortunately, when Clinton won, he turned inward and for the most part fell back on the security policy that had existed previously. 

NATO itself is a relic of an age that ended 30+ years ago. Europe itself is perfectly capable of defending itself (if it chooses to do so) at this point and US security interests are not meaningfully affected by what happens in Europe for the most part at this point (absent something extreme). Moreover, Europe has become something of a de facto adversary in how they behave toward the US in trade, foreign policy, and how they’ve organized themselves around utilizing the US security protections as a de facto subsidy for their social/welfare spending (e.g., look at the UK’s and Germany’s recent admission they may need to cut various social spending programs to accommodate the need for additional defense spending).

Still, for the most part the US hasn’t reached the point where it is willing to totally abandon NATO (although that would actually make a lot of sense when looked at dispassionately). The result is a change in how we address the overall defense/security strategy. The new approach is that we will leave Europe to be defended by Europe and focus our defense capabilities elsewhere where they best align with our security interests and those areas where Europe has no interest or limited capabilities. For instance, Europe’s ability to fight in the Pacific is extremely limited while we have significantly greater capabilities in that region. It makes sense to divvy up the various areas of concern based on who is best able to do what and has the greatest interests.

>> Literally anything that makes voting more difficult is voter suppression

> Most ridiculous thing I have ever heard.

It may sound ridiculous but it’s a well established fact. Literally any extra step or requirement you put on voting will reduce the turnout. 

Imagine you passed an arbitrary rule that you have to wear white socks when voting. This is not a particularly difficult bar to pass, but some people will miss or forget the rule change and turn up in the wrong socks. Some people will know the rule but can’t find clean white socks so won’t go. Some people will just be put off by the extra arbitrary requirement.

The exact same thing happens when you introduce extra requirements to getting mail in ballots, or require voter-ID for in person ballots. The amount of people who don’t vote under those conditions is orders of magnitude bigger than the amount of people who *can’t* meet the requirements.

You’re treating “16 weeks” like it was an actual negotiated settlement that would have locked everyone into 16 weeks nationally. It wasn’t, it was just the number attached to one state law that became the vehicle for attacking Roe/Casey.

If pro-choice groups had “accepted” that law, it wouldn’t magically freeze abortion law at 16 weeks. It would just mean that one pre-viability ban went unchallenged, which would invite the next state to try 12, then 10, then 6, then near-total bans. The issue wasn't “is 16 weeks the compromise?”, it was whether the pre-viability protection still existed at all.

So blaming Roe being overturned on “pro-choice people not accepting 16 weeks” is backwards. The 16-week number was incidental. The pro-life legal strategy was to use that case to destroy the constitutional rule underneath it. Once that rule was gone, there was no reason to think the result would stop at 16. That’s exactly why treating it as a clean compromise is not serious.

In the case of the fugitive slave act of 1850, the federal government mandated that any individual, including law enforcement, was required to report and capture escaped slaves and return them to their owners. Any person who was found helping escaped slaves is subject to a five-dollar fine and 6 months in prison.

Under these laws, there were crimes committed by people helping the escaped slave; local law enforcement, under the 1850 law, was required to arrest these people. This would be true even in the case of the escaped slave who was a victim of a crime.

I think the crux of our difference lies in notions around profit, and the purpose of business. The Friedman approach is that the sole purpose of companies is to make a profit. That profit is then primarily enjoyed by stakeholders, and stakeholders are primarily retired grandmas and grandpas living off their life savings. 

The related idea is that pure profit is pure good, and the more profit you make, the more value you add to society. There are clear refutations to this, like the tragedy of the commons, negative externalities, etc. ideally, the government creates a regulatory environment that protects the commons, internalizes negative externalities, promotes price transparency, breaks up monopolies etc. This regulatory environment is never perfect, so profit is never a perfect measure of value added to society, but it's the goal of free marketeers. 

I would agree that private companies are generally better at long term thinking than public companies, but Id consider that difference to be pretty small, and we'd likely disagree on the degree of this difference.

I also suspect that most of your criticisms of financialization are criticisms of crony capitalism, and if so then we're both in agreement, crony capitalism is really really bad. And it too is the same issue as above, an improper relationship and regulatory environment between business and government that leads to unideal outcomes.

The self-hating Westerners are part of the reason immigration can become problematic. In a healthy society, a certain level of immigration can be accommodated because those immigrant populations will eventually be assimilated into the native culture. Those parts of the immigrant culture which are compatible and valuable can be preserved and absorbed into the native culture, while aspect which are not compatible or valuable can be rejected. 

However, if the native population ceases to believe that its own culture is valuable, or, even worse, if people come to believe that their own culture is fundamentally evil and *shouldn’t* be preserved, then the mechanisms of assimilation will not operate, and eventually they will reverse until the native culture is replaced by or assimilated into the new dominant culture.

For a historical example of this sort of process, you just have to look at the rise of Christianity in the Roman Empire. Christianity didn’t conquer Rome by the sword. It became the dominant religion in the Roman world by a slow process of conversion. The Roman people themselves gradually ceased to believe in their own gods, and eventually there was no one left who even remembered a time when the Pantheon was anything other than a Christian church.

The problem in this for America is that politics is downstream of culture, as the famous saying goes. Laws are not self-enforcing, nor is the Constitution. In order for the freedoms guaranteed by our Constitution to be upheld, it is necessary for people to believe in the fundamental principles undergirding the Constitution. Those principles are themselves largely products of the Western cultural tradition, and they rely on Western assumptions for their support. If you lose Western culture, then so too goes the Liberty which most of us prize so dearly.

It's pretty simple for area rules. 

* Per population a district can be more than x ratio from another. This keeps from gaming teeny tiny or massive districts that aren't their size as a representation of people.

* Limit the number of sides to a polygon. You need to have polygon freedom to follow things like natural land features, but keeping a limit prevents ridiculous per street or block gaming.

* Compactness metrics. All districts should be their approximate blob shape, not long squished, snake-like, wildly turning and accommodating to a political goal. You basically compare the min and max width/height area against a circle, if it's too far out, it fails the test.

And when red states "gerrymander" they actually are drawing districts the way they were intended to be drawn -- by lumping together similar populations within reasonable, contiguous geography. Red states rarely end up looking like Illinois with the infamous squiggles. It happens on occasion, but the overwhelming majority of the time a red state is accused of "gerrymandering" it's because they're optimizing for fewest wasted votes. Democrats crowd into densely packed geographic areas where they run up the score to 80% vote share while Republican areas are generally more diverse and they only win by 55-60% margins. This means blue cities often get representation, but a red state that's split 60/40 will probably only be sending one or two reps while the rest of the districts voted red. So it's disproportionate. So even without gerrymandering, we get sued into oblivion for drawing maps they way the framers intended them to be drawn.

For a while Missouri had a ridiculous map where St. Louis city was split into three sections and lumped with juuuuust enough rural voters that their margins were 55D/45R. You had super posh Clayton and bumfuck rural JeffCo with the same rep. It was insane. But the number or reps we sent to DC was more proportional for how the state votes, but it ended up disenfranchising a lot more people. The newer maps are more reasonable since the main district of St. Louis is located on the population center. Now the number of people happy with their representative is maximized, but the Democrats are furious that their gerrymander was removed and they get fewer congressional seats.

That being said, one of the most common ways crony capitalism happens is when established companies lobby for regulation in their industry that either their company is uniquely suited to comply with, or is uniquely onerous for new entrants. Its one reason why corporations seem to get bigger and more consolidated over time, only large established companies can profitably comply with every regulation. Lots of other crony capitalism examples, like stadium subsidies, tariffs, tax breaks for companies, etc. 
  
I think long-term planning is much less of an issue recently, as US companies are transitioning away from dividend models. It was Amazon I believe that didn't take a profit for years and years because they were investing so much, and eventually shareholders started to revolt, so they let off the gas for a brief moment, took a large profit, and the shareholders who revolted lost out on a huge market rally. This sort of thing happened enough that it's kind of the norm now that companies don't have to give out dividends and don't have to take profits either as long as it's clear they're investing heavily in future growth. 

And I'm certainly open to the idea of state monopolies in certain industries. USPS is a good example, mail delivery is basic infrastructure that rural people need even though it's basically impossible to do rural delivery without taking a loss. 

Utilities I'm half and half on. Texas' model feels uniquely competitive and privatized, and it's shown lower reliability during weather events, but overall prices are pretty good, and more importantly energy growth in Texas is off the charts, and largely renewables. No, government doesn't automatically "work for everyone" just because it's a representative democracy. That's the naive assumption of a lot on the far left, funnily enough. In practice government works for the loudest, best-organized, and most connected interests and usually often at the expense of the quiet, independent, or anti-government citizen. Your logging uncle in a cabin in the woods working a 9-5 and reading old books still gets taxed at gunpoint for a government he explicitly doesn't want. He has no real opt-out. That's the fundamental difference.

On monopolies/utilities/ISPs: Yes, some sectors have limited competition and high barriers- these businesses are expensive to own and manage; the key is that "not practical for most people to go off-grid" doesn't turn a voluntary transaction into coercion. You can choose different providers where they exist, pressure for deregulation to increase competition, or yes you absolutely *can* live more simply. The IRS doesn't give you those options. They show up with force if you reject their "service." That's the distinction between state power and market power. Try to think about this honestly, please.

The core conservative principle is this: *coercion* requires strict limits and constant skepticism. Government has a monopoly on legitimate violence. Corporations do not have this power or ability in any fashion, internet hyperbole notwithstanding. I'll take a hundred greedy businesses I can ignore, boycott, or compete with over one unaccountable bureaucracy that can fine, regulate, or imprison me.

Your big question is kinda a farce. Do wealthy elites have outsized influence on policy? Of course they do. So do unions, environmental NGOs, trial lawyers, universities, and identity groups. That's reality in a massive $6+ trillion government and a world where freedom of assembly and speech are valued: more money definitionally means more speech and more access. The honest fix isn't pretending "democracy" magically purifies this: democratic socialism is just *voting* to increase the size and scope of a deadly cancer instead of it just getting bigger organically. Neither one is good.

The fix is and always has been shrinking the prize worth capturing. Reduce the scope, power, and budget of the federal government so there's less for any elites whether wealthy, culturally empowered, media-boosted, or otherwise to hijack and utilize. Problem solved.

So any time you say that the government should mandate something, you are starting with the assumption that the government is correct. 
If you start with the assumption that the government is trying its best (which, frankly is a stretch) but is also fallible, then logic says that the government requirements should actually be recommendations, and individuals should be able to override those. 

The constitution calls out the responsibility of the govt to PROMOTE the general welfare, not to mandate it. Anything else is a gross overstep of powers. I know I’m conflating policy with science. But every era of the modern world has believed they were the most scientifically knowledgeable. It’s naive of us to think we’ve got it all figured out now. In the 1820s doctors believed that women’s uteruses would fall out if the train went too fast. In the 1920s, doctors believed smoking was good for you. In the 2020s doctors believed that injecting you with chemicals would make you live longer. I’m not saying that vaccines are bad and don’t work, I’m saying I have enough doubt in the system to question whether something should be mandatory under the law. 

Ultimately I believe in individual rights over government powers. TL;DR: I'm not entirely sure where I'm going with all of this other than to say I wish there were a much greater emphasis on long-term planning at the highest levels of leadership across both government and industry.

We're 100% in agreement on crony capitalism. That stuff is incredibly corrosive to the overall health of any industry where it takes root.

We're also aligned on the difference between public- and private-sector long-term planning. Doing serious, professional long-term planning is expensive. Because of that, it wouldn't surprise me if private companies are better at it than public ones and/or  governmental organizations, but not nearly as much better as people often assume.

Have you ever thought about the idea that corporations, as entities, are incentivized to privatize as much profit as possible while socializing as much of the cost and consequence as possible? At its core, it's another version of the tragedy of the commons. One of the clearest examples is toxic waste dumping into public waterways. Proper disposal is expensive. It's often cheaper to dump the waste illegally and then hire lawyers to fight the resulting legal battles. There are plenty of other examples, but that one illustrates the dynamic pretty well.

It's been interesting because in my MBA program we've been discussing the incentives that drive corporate behavior, starting with shareholder supremacy. That idea is increasingly being challenged by stakeholder theory, but stakeholder theory hasn't really been translated into a dominant legal doctrine yet. Shareholder-focused thinking still heavily influences corporate governance structures.

I've also been thinking a lot about industries that perhaps shouldn't be fully subject to market forces. Things like the 16 critical national infrastructure sectors. Some industries are so vital to public functioning and national security that there may need to be a government-backed option to provide a stable baseline level of service.

Take energy, for example. During supply shocks, oil companies can post record profits while consumers face rapidly rising prices. On one hand, that makes perfect sense from a market perspective. Oil is a global commodity, and when supply is constrained, existing suppliers benefit. Those outcomes follow basic market fundamentals.

On the other hand, it's easy to see why many people view that situation as price gouging. The logic of the market and the public perception of fairness are not always the same thing. The same dynamic can be found in almost any commodity market. What is rational from a pricing perspective can still create outcomes that feel deeply unfair to the people experiencing them.

So "conservatives hate government employees" is exactly the kind of oversimplification he traffics in, but let's leave Trump out of it and try for clarity on your central question, starting with the framing.

You are asking a question -- whether conservatives hate government employees -- that can only be answered usefully with a generic claim. A generic claim is one that describes what is characteristic or typical of a kind of thing rather than making an absolute statement about every individual instance. For example, "teenagers are glued to their phone" is a fair characterization of that generation, not an absolute, so answering "but I know of this teenager who hardly ever uses her phone" would be bad faith pedantry.

If someone says, "conservatives tend to be skeptical of government workers," they are expressing a general orientation, not making a statement about every conservative. So coming back with, "but my uncle Bob is a conservative and he's a postal worker" or "not all government workers . . ." would also be bad faith pedantry and not get you any closer to understanding the issue. And, it's possible Uncle Bob formed his negative opinion of government workers through experience.

Anyway, not trying to put words in your mouth. I'm just making sure everything is framed properly because clarity is more valuable than agreement.

With that framing in place: I prefer to say that conservatives are more impressed by private sector workers than government workers. And this comes from their observations, not some kind of arbitrary tribal contempt.

Government employment tends to insulate workers from consequences. It's notoriously difficult to fire a federal employee, compensation and benefits are generous relative to comparable private sector roles, and there's no market mechanism punishing inefficiency or incompetence the way it does a business owner. Ever heard the expression, "good enough for government work?" Whether or not that perception is fully accurate is a separate debate, but *that's* what is behind the general conservative skepticism of government employees. And it's less "I hate these people" and more "these jobs aren't part of a system that rewards merit."

As for the Vought quote: it is more of a distraction as far as your general question is concerned, so not very useful. Vought isn't talking about government employees in general; he's specifically targeting the so-called "administrative state" or "deep state," both of which refer to areas of government where unelected career bureaucrats wield too much authority relative to their accountability. While it's true that essentially all "deep state" figures are government employees, it's not true that all government employees are deep state. Vought isn't talking about the DMV clerk who is painting her nails while a line of fifty people wait to get their license renewed. The intensity of contempt you see in Vought's quote, whatever you may think of it, is not representative of what conservatives in general think of government employees.

So, do many conservatives have a generic skepticism about government work? Yes, myself included, for the reasons above. Does that rise to *hatred* of the people doing it? Not for most conservatives. And Vought's quote is worthy of a separate discussion but a red herring for this one.

I think the crux of our difference lies in notions around profit, and the purpose of business. The Friedman approach is that the sole purpose of companies is to make a profit. That profit is then primarily enjoyed by stakeholders, and stakeholders are primarily retired grandmas and grandpas living off their life savings. 

The related idea is that pure profit is pure good, and the more profit you make, the more value you add to society. There are clear refutations to this, like the tragedy of the commons, negative externalities, etc. ideally, the government creates a regulatory environment that protects the commons, internalizes negative externalities, promotes price transparency, breaks up monopolies etc. This regulatory environment is never perfect, so profit is never a perfect measure of value added to society, but it's the goal of free marketeers. 

I would agree that private companies are generally better at long term thinking than public companies, but Id consider that difference to be pretty small, and we'd likely disagree on the degree of this difference.

The self-hating Westerners are part of the reason immigration can become problematic. In a healthy society, a certain level of immigration can be accommodated because those immigrant populations will eventually be assimilated into the native culture. Those parts of the immigrant culture which are compatible and valuable can be preserved and absorbed into the native culture, while aspect which are not compatible or valuable can be rejected. 

However, if the native population ceases to believe that its own culture is valuable, or, even worse, if people come to believe that their own culture is fundamentally evil and *shouldn’t* be preserved, then the mechanisms of assimilation will not operate, and eventually they will reverse until the native culture is replaced by or assimilated into the new dominant culture.

For a historical example of this sort of process, you just have to look at the rise of Christianity in the Roman Empire. Christianity didn’t conquer Rome by the sword. It became the dominant religion in the Roman world by a slow process of conversion. The Roman people themselves gradually ceased to believe in their own gods, and eventually there was no one left who even remembered a time when the Pantheon was anything other than a Christian church.
